era_context: The mid-8th century CE is marked by major political upheavals: the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE) continues to devastate Tang China, the Abbasid Caliphate consolidates power following the 750 CE revolution, and the Frankish Kingdom expands under Pepin III. This is a period of significant military conflict and political reorganization across Eurasia.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion Reaches Peak; Tang Dynasty in Crisis
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which began in 755 CE, reaches its peak in 756 CE. An Lushan's forces capture the Tang capital of Chang'an (modern Xi'an) and establish a rival state. Emperor Xuanzong flees to Sichuan, and his son Suzong assumes the throne. The rebellion causes massive civilian casualties, economic disruption, and territorial fragmentation. The Tang Dynasty faces an existential crisis as rebel forces control significant portions of northern China.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The capture of Chang'an and the succession of Suzong are firmly dated to 756 CE in Chinese chronicles. This is one of the most significant events of the year.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate Consolidates Power; Administrative Reorganization
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) continues to consolidate power and suppress remaining Umayyad loyalists. This period sees the integration of Persian administrative practices into the Islamic state and the strengthening of the Khorasan Guard. The Abbasid state is establishing itself as a more centralized, bureaucratic empire compared to the decentralized Umayyad system.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Abbasid consolidation is documented in later Islamic historiography, but specific events in 756 CE are often undated or conflated with broader trends of the 750s–760s.

events.title: Frankish Expansion under Pepin III; Papal Relations
events.region: Frankish Kingdom and Papal States (Western Europe)
events.description: Pepin III continues military campaigns to expand Frankish power and consolidate control over neighboring territories. The Frankish kingdom strengthens its ties with the Papacy, establishing the foundation for the Carolingian Empire's later role as protector of the Church. Pepin's military campaigns against Aquitaine, Saxony, and other regions extend Frankish hegemony.
events.certainty_note: Frankish military campaigns and papal relations are documented in Carolingian-era chronicles and papal records, though specific events in 756 CE are sometimes undated.

events.title: Byzantine-Arab Naval Conflicts in the Mediterranean
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V continues naval warfare against Arab forces in the Mediterranean. Byzantine fleets engage in raids and defensive operations against Arab naval power. These conflicts are part of the broader struggle for control of Mediterranean trade routes and coastal territories.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts are attested in Byzantine chronicles, but specific engagements in 756 CE are often undated or conflated with broader campaigns.

events.title: Umayyad Survivor Abd al-Rahman I Establishes Emirate in Iberia
events.description: Abd al-Rahman I, a survivor of the Umayyad dynasty, establishes the Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba in Iberia (Al-Andalus) after fleeing the Abbasid Revolution. This marks the beginning of a new Umayyad state independent of the Abbasid Caliphate. The establishment of this emirate creates a significant Islamic power center in Western Europe and establishes a rival to Abbasid authority.
key_figures: Abd al-Rahman I (founder of Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh)

events.certainty_note: Abd al-Rahman I's establishment of the Umayyad Emirate is documented in later Islamic historiography. The date 756 CE is the traditional date for the founding, though some sources place it slightly earlier or later. The general event is confirmed, but precise dating may vary by ±1–2 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Abd al-Rahman I's establishment of the Umayyad Emirate in Iberia is sometimes placed in 755 CE or 757 CE rather than 756 CE, depending on the source. The traditional date is 756 CE, but scholarly consensus allows for ±1–2 years of uncertainty. No other major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 756 CE is moderate. Chinese chronicles provide detailed coverage of the An Lushan Rebellion and the capture of Chang'an, making this one of the most significant events of the year. Islamic sources document the establishment of the Umayyad Emirate in Iberia, though dating varies slightly. Frankish and Byzantine sources are sparser for this year. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political history; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely absent. The year 756 CE is marked as significant in Chinese and Islamic historiography but less so in Western European sources.

graph_edges.note: The capture of Chang'an and the crisis of the Tang Dynasty eventually led to the rebellion's suppression in 763 CE
Abbasid consolidation continued throughout al-Mansur's reign, culminating in the founding of Baghdad
Pepin III's military campaigns and papal relations established the foundation for Charlemagne's expansionist policies
Abd al-Rahman I's establishment of the Umayyad Emirate was a direct consequence of the Abbasid Revolution
The Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba eventually became the Umayyad Caliphate in 929 CE
